# SECTION 5.11 - LOGGING PROCESS DATA

## 5.11 Logging process data
Logging data is automatically collected and can be used to optimize the process based on the process results.

**CRITICAL LIMITATION**: This logging function is only available in TSC-2, not on a touchscreen.

## 5.11.1 TSC-2 (only)

### Step-by-Step Procedure:

**Step 1)** Return to the main overview screen

**Step 2)** Select 'Data' in the navigation bar to access the data logging screen

**Step 3)** Select Log Data

**Step 4)** Double click on a predefined dataset or use the New button to create one

**Step 5)** Select the data points of interest

### Data Point Selection Interface

- **OK** and **Cancel** buttons

**Step 7)** Select History Lot to search all logging data for matching Lot IDs.

**NOTE**: use wildcards to extend the search

# Technical Manual Extract: Pages 41-45
**Document**: 1179B/1479B/2179B/179B Flow Controller Manual

---

## PAGE 41 - CHAPTER 5: OPERATION (ANALOG UNITS)

### Override the Control Valve

The valve override feature enables the control valve to be fully opened (purged) or closed independent of the set point command signal:

**Valve Control Commands:**
- **To open the valve**: apply a TTL low to pin 4 or connect pin 4 to pin 5 (analog ground)
- **To close the valve**: apply a TTL low to pin 3 or connect pin 3 to pin 5 (analog ground)

### Priority of Commands

The 1179 flow controller executes commands based on a hierarchical command structure. The highest priority command is **Valve Open**, followed by **Valve Close**, and finally the **Set Point Control**. Therefore, if the flow controller is operating under Set Point Control, you can send a Valve Open command to force the valve to the full open position.

**CRITICAL NOTE**: When both the Valve Close and Valve Open pins are pulled down, the Valve Open command takes precedence and the valve is moved to the open position.

---

## PAGE 42 - INTENTIONALLY BLANK PAGE

This page intentionally left blank

---

## PAGE 43 - CHAPTER 6: OPERATION (PROFIBUS UNITS)

### Functions

Beside the normal flow control mode the units equipped with Profibus provide a number of helpful functions and useful information.

### Overview - Available Functions:

**Control Functions:**
- Valve Override
- Auto Zero
- Totalizer, gas counter
- Gas Correction
- 15 Calibration Tables, 15 points each
- 4 Limit Alarms
- Run Hours Counter
- Remaining Time to Calibration Observation (Calibration Cycle Count Down)

**Measurement Functions:**
- Temperature Measurement
- Valve Drive Level
- Status LED
- Signal Filter (flow signal)

**User Configuration:**
- User Span setting
- User Zero setting
- User Tag

### Report Functions:
Complete device information available including:
- Calibration Date
- Type
- Model
- Manufacturer
- Serial No.
- Firmware/Hardware Revision
- Product Code
- Date of Factory Calibration
- Full Scale Range
- Engineering Unit
- Standard Temperature
- Standard Pressure
- Valve Type
- Valve Power Off Mode

---

## PAGE 44 - DETAILED PROFIBUS OPERATIONS

### Valve Override

**Analog Mode Override:**
By means of an analog override signal applied to the respective pins at the connector ANALOG the control valve can be driven completely open or closed, independent of the setpoint command signal (only in analog mode). Refer to figure 9 and its explanations for the detailed description.

**Profibus Mode Override:**
In Profibus mode the override however is only possible via the bus:

**VALVE_OVERRIDE Parameter Settings:**
- **NORMAL**: Set to allow the mass flow controller establishing a flow in accordance to the setpoint command FLOW_SETPOINT
- **FLOW_OFF**: Set to completely close the control valve
- **PURGE**: Set to completely open the control valve. This command may be used to purge or vent the instrument or the system

### Auto Zero

**Auto Zero Conditions:**
If VALVE_OVERRIDE is equal to FLOW_OFF and THERMAL_MASS_FLOW_RATE is smaller than 5% of full scale, the flag AUTOZERO (0 to 1 transition) will take the actual sensor signal as zero and will subtract this value from all future measurements (refer also to USER_ZERO).

### Alarm Limits

**Limit Parameters:**
- **LOW_TRIP_POINT** and **HIGH_TRIP_POINT** define the limits for LOW_LIMIT_ALARM and HIGH_LIMIT_ALARM flags
- **Hysteresis**: 0.5% hysteresis for each limit switch
- **Hysteresis Distribution**: The hysteresis of 0.5% is divided in a slope 2.5% above and another slope 2.5% below the defined limit

### Temperature Measurement

**Internal Temperature:**
The internal temperature of the device is measured in °C and given as INTERNAL_TEMP.

### Valve Drive Level

**VALVE_DRIVE_LEVEL Parameters:**
- **0%**: Valve is closed
- **100%**: Valve is fully opened (e.g. at the PURGE command)
- **Typical Operating Range**: 40…60% with new units under normal operating conditions

**Factors Affecting Valve Drive Level:**
The value depends on many parameters:
- Pressure conditions
- Temperature
- Contamination
- Age
- May change during operation even at the same flow rate

---

## PAGE 45 - ADVANCED PROFIBUS PARAMETERS

### User Span / Gas Correction

**Accuracy Tuning:**
- **USER_SPAN parameter**: Should be used for tuning the flow controller's accuracy
- **Gas Application Method 1**: For application of a specific gas the nominal full scale (e.g. N2) may be multiplied by the GCF of the gas and the parameter FULL_SCALE_RNG may be set with the result
- **Gas Application Method 2**: Set the parameter GAS_CORRECTION to the GCF of the gas (full functionality only)

### User Zero

**USER_ZERO Parameter:**
The parameter USER_ZERO may be set to the flow sensors offset. (See also AUTO ZERO)

### Filter

**Filter Specifications:**
- **Filter Type**: Single-pole low pass filter
- **Adjustable Parameter**: Settling time of the filter (FILTER_SETTLING)
- **Example**: If a step input (magnitude 100) is applied to the filter with a settling time of 100 second, the response out of the filter will be within 2% of the final value (98%) in 100 second
- **Default Value**: 0 seconds (no filtering)

### Gas Tables

**Gas Table Configuration Options:**

**Method 1**: Gas tables may be switched off by setting the default table to 15 (i.e. no table is used) and by selecting the default table

**Method 2**: By programming a 0 for POINT_NUM (ref. to Calibration Table at the end of this chapter; function (y=x)) and selecting it

**Method 3**: By selecting a table with no (zero) points

**Backup System:**
A backup of the Gas Tables is generated on Factory Setup. This backup is used for reset the tables.

### Soft Start Rate

**SOFT_START_RATE Parameter:**
- **Function**: A change in the set point is performed in a slope, which is defined by SOFT_START_RATE
- **Factory Setting**: Each controller is factory set to its optimum performance

---

**Model Numbers Covered**: 1179B/1479B/2179B/179B

**Pin Assignments (Analog Mode):**
- Pin 3: Valve Close command (TTL low)
- Pin 4: Valve Open command (TTL low)

- Auto Zero activation: <5% of full scale
- Alarm hysteresis: 0.5% total (±2.5% around setpoint)
- Typical valve drive level: 40-60% for new units

**Calibration Capabilities:**
- 15 Calibration Tables available
- 15 points per calibration table
- Factory backup system for gas tables

**Temperature Units**: °C (Celsius) for internal temperature measurement

## PAGE 63: CHAPTER 7 - THEORY OF OPERATION

### Header Information
- Document: 1179B/1479B/2179B/179B Chapter 7: Theory of Operation
- Page: 63

### Technique of Measurement and Control, Electronics

The design of the 1179B flow controller incorporates:
- an advanced flow sensor
- a new control valve
- an optimized bypass

The latest generation two-element sensing circuit provides accurate, repeatable performance even in low flow ranges (< 10 sccm). Low temperature effect from ambient temperature change and a low attitude sensitivity effect are also ensured. 

The newly optimized sensor/bypass arrangement minimizes the flow splitting error for gases with different densities, which dramatically improves measurement accuracy when gases other than the calibration gas are used.

The surface mount digital, processor controlled electronic circuitry allows optimum adjustment of the sensing and signal conditioning circuitry and provides tuned flow control for fast response to any set point in common with excellent stability.

### Figure 18: Assembly Diagram Description
**Visual components labeled:**
- Electronic Board (top component with visible circuit elements)
- Valve Housing with Solenoid (middle section)
- O-Ring (sealing component)
- Bypass (left flow path)
- Valve Plug (center valve component)  
- Seal Cones (sealing elements)
- Sensor (measurement component)

### Technical Specifications

#### Flow Controller Type 1179B Performance:
- **Control Range:** 2% to 100% of full scale
- **Accuracy:** ± (0.5% of reading + 0.2% of F.S.) for both analog units and Profibus versions
- **Setpoint Input:** analog signal or digital command (Profibus version)

---

## PAGE 64: FLOW PATH AND MEASUREMENT TECHNIQUE

### Header Information
- Document: Chapter 7: Theory of Operation 1179B/1479B/2179B/179B
- Page: 64

### Flow Path

Upon entering the flow controller, the gas stream passes first through the metering section of the instrument for its mass flow to be measured. The gas moves on through the control valve for its rate of flow to be regulated according to the given set point, and then exits the instrument at the established rate of flow.

#### The metering section consists of one of the following:
- **A sensor tube for ranges < 10 sccm (N₂ equivalent)**
- **A sensor tube and parallel bypass for ranges > 10 sccm (N₂ equivalent)**

The geometry of the sensor tube, in conjunction with the specified full scale flow rate, ensures fully developed laminar flow in the sensing region. The bypass elements, in those instruments containing them, are specifically matched to the characteristics of the sensor tube to achieve a laminar flow splitting ratio which remains constant throughout each range.

### Measurement Technique

The flow measurement is based on differential heat transfer between temperature sensing heater elements which are attached symmetrically to the sensor tube. This senses the thermal mass movement which is converted to mass flow via the specific heat, Cp, of the gas. The resulting signal is then amplified, digitalized and linearized. The corrected digital signal is then transferred to the control section (controllers only) and also converted into a 0 – 5 V analog signal.

Analog versions of the units described herein provide just the analog flow signal, Profibus versions provide both the digital information and the analog signal.

The measurement principle of keeping temperatures constant results in much shorter response time than conventional principles.

### Control Circuitry

In the digital control section the flow rate is compared to the setpoint value and a control signal (digital) is generated.

The digital control signal is then conditioned by a PID-algorithm, optimized for fastest controlling and finally fed into the control circuitry which steers the solenoid control valve. The digital control reduces overshoots to a minimum and for completely regulating the flow until the difference from the setpoint is zero. 

#### Performance Specifications:
- **Typical settle time:** 0.8 s
- **Note:** for faster tuning contact MKS

#### Control Valve Operation:
- **Default State:** Normally Closed (N.C.) when no power is applied
- **Control Method:** Controlling flow is done by levitating the valve plug from the valve orifice
- **Mounting:** The plug is mounted at the front end of the solenoid armature

---

## PAGE 65: CONTROL VALVE DETAILED SPECIFICATIONS

### Header Information
- Document: 1179B/1479B/2179B/179B Chapter 7: Theory of Operation
- Page: 65

### Control Valve

The control valve is a specially designed solenoid driven valve. The armature is suspended by two radial springs. This design provides frictionless movement and thus precise control. Mounted at the front end of the cylindrical armature is the valve plug which incorporates the seal disc of:
- **Viton**
- **Kel-F** 
- **Teflon** 
(ref. to Appendix A, Specifications)

By preload force of the two above mentioned springs the seal disc is pressed against the valve orifice, closing it's flow channel. Therefore the valve is closed when not activated. It is a "Normally Closed" (N.C.) valve.

#### Orifice Specifications:
The inside diameter of the orifice determines the conductance. Each flow controller incorporates a valve orifice with a conductance in accordance to the full scale range. 

#### Standard Operating Conditions:
The valve orifice in standard units is sized that with a pressure difference between inlet and outlet fitting of **typical 0.7 bar to 2.75 bar** the specified full scale flow rate will be achieved. (related to air or nitrogen). 

For more information refer to **Appendix A, Specifications**.

#### Special Application Configurations:

**Low Pressure Applications:**
- For special applications with low pressure conditions, e.g. vaporizer sources, configurations can be provided
- In this case the valve will have an orifice with higher conductance (=larger diameter of the flow channel)
- Mass flow controllers for applications where only **200 mbar (or less)** are available have been realized

**High Pressure Applications:**
- At high line pressures in combination with high flow rates it may be necessary to have an orifice with smaller conductance installed

#### Important Note:
**The mass flow monitor 179B has no valve!**

## PAGE 77 - CHAPTER 11: TYPE 179B, 1179B, 1479B, 2179B (COMPARISON)

### Chapter 11: Type 179B, 1179B, 1479B, 2179B (Comparison)

#### Type 179B
The type 179B is the standard mass flow meter and provides the base for the mass flow controllers type 1179, 1479 and 2179. The imprint on the aluminium housing is MASS-FLO METER. The flow path is completely metal sealed. Therefore the meter is inherently an all metal sealed unit.

#### Type 1179B
The Type 1179B is the standard version of the MKS Mass-Flo® Controller series. Compared to the flow monitor type 179B it employs an integrated control valve, solenoid driven, and a digital control electronics. There is just one O-ring seal in the valve body, all other process gas wetted surfaces are metal sealed or even welded.

The control valve is normally closed, e.g. when no power or a zero setpoint signal is applied. The sealing material of the valve plug is typically Viton, Kel-F or Teflon which provides a closed conductance which is sufficiently small for many application. Nevertheless it must be pointed out that for reliable and completely closing a positive shut off valve is strongly recommended.

#### Type 1479B
The Type 1479B is the completely metal sealed type 1179B, meaning that also the one O-ring seal is replaced by a metal C-seal. The valve plug seal disc is Kel-F or Teflon, depending of the range.

#### Type 2179B
The Type 2179B consists of a mass flow controller 1179B with a positive shut off valve closely welded to the outlet. This is the recommended configuration for closing flow at minimum leak rate with minimized dead volume between shutoff valve and control valve. The welded connection prevents an additional connection by fittings with potential leak.

The shutoff valve is an all metal sealed diaphragm valve (material: 316L VAR SST and Elgiloy) with a PCTFE valve seat.

The complete stick is mounted on a common base plate.

**Document Header:** 1179B/1479B/2179B/179B Chapter 11: Type 179B, 1179B, 1479B, 2179B (Comparison)
**Page Number:** 77

---

## PAGE 78 - PNEUMATIC POSITIVE SHUTOFF VALVE

### Pneumatic Positive Shutoff Valve

To open the shutoff valve pressurized air at 4,1 bar(g) to 8,2 bar(g) is required. The air port is a 1/8" – 27 NPT female thread. To close the valve the air pressure must be released.

The thread connection should be done using Teflon tape thus reducing friction and avoiding damages to the thread.

### Note
**[Note Icon Present]** Throughout this handbook all three types of mass flow controllers, namely 1179B, 1479B and 2179B will be expressed by type 1179B in common. The types 1479B and 2179B will only be mentioned by their specific name where it is necessary to avoid confusion.

**Document Header:** Chapter 11: Type 179B, 1179B, 1479B, 2179B (Comparison) 1179B/1479B/2179B/179B

## PAGE 67 - CHAPTER 8: GAS CORRECTION FACTOR (GCF)

### Chapter 8: Gas Correction Factor (GCF)

#### The Gas Correction Factor (GCF):
A Gas Correction Factor (GCF) is used to indicate the ratio of flow rates of different gases which will produce the same output signal from a mass flow meter / controller. The GCF is a function of specific heat, density, and the molecular structure of the gases. Nitrogen (N2) is normally used as the baseline gas (GCF = 1) since flow meters and controllers are usually calibrated with nitrogen.

**GCF (N2) = 1**

Appendix C lists the gas correction factors for many commonly used pure gases. If the gas you are using is not listed in there, you must calculate its GCF. The equations for calculating gas correction factors are described below.

#### How To Calculate the GCF for Pure Gases
To calculate the Gas Correction Factor for any pure gas (X), use the following equation:

**GCFx = (0.3106 × s) / (ρx × cpx)**

**Where:**
- **GCFx** = gas correction factor for gas X
- **0.3106** = (standard density of nitrogen) • (specific heat of nitrogen)
- **s** = molecular structure correction factor where S equals:
  - **1.030** for monoatomic gases
  - **1.000** for diatomic gases
  - **0.941** for triatomic gases
  - **0.880** for polyatomic gases
- **dx** = standard density of gas X, in g/l (at 0° C and 1013,25 mbar)
- **cpx** = specific heat of gas X, in cal/g° C

---

## PAGE 68 - GAS CORRECTION FACTOR CALCULATIONS

#### How To Calculate the GCF for Gas Mixtures
For gas mixtures, the calculated Gas Correction Factor is not simply the weighted average of each component's GCF. Instead, the GCF (relative to nitrogen) is calculated by the following equation:

**GCFm = (0.3106 × (a1s1 + a2s2 + ....ansn)) / (a1ρ1cp1 + a2ρ2cp2 + .... anρncpn)**

**Where:**
- **GCFm** = gas correction factor for a gas mixture
- **0.3106** = (standard density of nitrogen) (specific heat of nitrogen)
- **a1, a2,...an** = fractional flow of gases 1 through n
  - **Note:** a1 through an must add up to 1.0
- **s1, s2,...sn** = Molecular Structure correction factor for gases 1 through n where S equals:
  - **1.030** for monatomic gases
  - **1.000** for diatomic gases
  - **0.941** for triatomic gases
  - **0.880** for polyatomic gases
- **d1 through dn** = standard density for gases 1 through n, in g/l (at 0° C and 760 mmHg)
- **cp1 through cpn** = specific heat of gases 1 through n, in cal/g° C

#### CRITICAL NOTES:
**Note 1.** When using the GCF, the accuracy of the flow reading may vary by ± 5%, however, the repeatability will remain ± 0,2% of FS.

**Note 2.** The linearity and accuracy may be improved by calibrating the unit with the process gas or using a gas with equivalent properties (surrogate gas). Contact MKS for more information.

**Note 3.** All MKS readouts have gas correction adjustment controls to provide direct readout.

---

## PAGE 69 - EXAMPLE CALCULATION AND TEMPERATURE CORRECTION

#### Example
Calculate the GCF for a gas mixture of argon (gas 1) flowing at 150 sccm and nitrogen (gas 2) flowing at 50 sccm, where:

**Argon (Ar) Properties:**
- a1 = 150/200 = 0.75
- s1 = 1.030
- d1 = 1.782 g/l
- cp1 = 0.1244 cal/g ° C

**Nitrogen (N2) Properties:**
- a2 = 50/200 = 0.25
- s2 = 1.000
- d2 = 1.250 g/l
- cp2 = 0.2485 cal/g ° C

**Calculation Steps:**
GCFM = (0.3106) [(0.75)(1.030) + (0.25)(1.000)] / [(0.75)(1.782)(0.1244) + (0.25)(1.250)(0.2485)]

= (0.3106) [(0.7725) + (0.25)] / [(0.1663) + (0.0777)]

= (0.3106) (1.0225) / 0.244

= 0.3176 / 0.244

**GCFM = 1.302**

#### Mass Flow Rate at a Different Reference Temperature
The equations for calculating the GCF assume that the MFC was calibrated at a reference temperature of 0° C (~273,15 K). If you want to read the mass flow as if the MFC was calibrated at a different reference temperature, adjust the calculated GCF value using the following equation:

**GCFx = GCF × (Tx/TN)**

**Where:**
- **Tx** = actual reference temperature in Kelvin K
- **TN** = international standard temperature 273.15 K ( = 0° C)

---

## PAGE 70 - DIRECT READING AND VOLTAGE DIVIDER

#### Direct Reading of the Analog Output Signal

If a direct reading of output is desirable, and the readout equipment has no GCF potentiometer, a proper input divider may be constructed.

**Example:** Using a mass flow controller, full scale range 500 sccm N2, full scale signal is 5 VDC and a process gas CCl4. A flow rate of 100 sccm CCl4 shall be converted into 1 VDC.

As the gas correction factor of CCl4 is 0,31 the flow signal must be divided 3.22 to 1.00 volts.

#### VOLTAGE DIVIDER CIRCUIT DIAGRAM:
**Figure 19: Voltage Divider**

**Circuit Components:**
- Input: 3.22 V
- Resistor 1: 22.2 K ohms
- Resistor 2: 10 K ohms  
- Output: 1.00 V (= 100sccm)

**Circuit Description:** Simple voltage divider circuit with two resistors in series. Input voltage of 3.22V is divided down to 1.00V output using the resistor network of 22.2K and 10K ohms.

#### FINAL NOTE:
**Note:** All MKS readouts have gas correction adjustment controls to provide direct readout. The analog setpoint output signal is generated accordingly.

---

## COMPLETE TECHNICAL SPECIFICATIONS SUMMARY:

### Model Numbers Covered:
- 1179B
- 1479B  
- 2179B
- 179B

- **Accuracy with GCF:** ± 5%
- **Repeatability:** ± 0.2% of FS
- **Reference Temperature:** 0° C (273.15 K)
- **Reference Pressure:** 1013.25 mbar (760 mmHg)
- **Baseline Gas:** Nitrogen (N2) with GCF = 1
- **Full Scale Signal:** 5 VDC
- **Standard Density Units:** g/l
- **Specific Heat Units:** cal/g° C

- **Monoatomic gases:** 1.030

- **Triatomic gases:** 0.941
- **Polyatomic gases:** 0.880

### Reference Constants:

# COMPLETE TECHNICAL EXTRACTION - MFC Manual Pages 71-75

## CHAPTER 9: MFC SIZING GUIDELINES (Pages 71-72)

### General Requirements
**To select the correct MFC for an application, you must determine the:**
- flow controller range
- appropriate valve configuration

**Flow Controller Range Dependencies:**
- Desired flow rate 
- Gas correction factor for the gas to be used
- MKS states flow controller ranges based on flow rate of nitrogen
- Flow rate for other gases may vary

**Valve Configuration Dependencies:**
- Flow range
- Inlet pressure  
- Differential pressure across the unit
- Density of the gas
- Proper valve configurations established for all standard flow ranges flowing nitrogen under standard operating pressures
- Configurations suitable for virtually all gases and pressure conditions

### Determining the Flow Controller Range

**Type 1179 Controller Available Ranges:**
- 10 sccm (N₂ equivalent)
- 20 sccm (N₂ equivalent)  
- 50 sccm (N₂ equivalent)
- 100 sccm (N₂ equivalent)
- 200 sccm (N₂ equivalent)
- 500 sccm (N₂ equivalent)
- 1000 sccm (N₂ equivalent)
- 2000 sccm (N₂ equivalent)
- 5000 sccm (N₂ equivalent)
- 10000 sccm (N₂ equivalent)
- 20000 sccm (N₂ equivalent)

**Selection Process:**
Must determine the flow rate of nitrogen that is equivalent to the flow rate of the desired gas. Calculate the ratio of the GCF of nitrogen (1.00) to the GCF of the desired gas (refer to Appendix C)

**Example Calculation:**
Flow rate needed: 250 sccm of argon (Ar)

**Step 1:** Find the Gas Correction Factor of Ar
- GCF for Ar = 1.39

**Step 2:** Insert the GCF of Ar in formula:
**(x) = (Argon flow rate) x GCF (N₂) : GCF (Ar)**

Where x is the equivalent flow rate of nitrogen (sccm)

**Calculation:**
(1.00)/(1.39) = (x)/(250 sccm Ar)
(x) = 180 sccm N₂

**Result:** A flow rate of 250 sccm of argon will produce a flow rate equivalent to 180 sccm of N₂. This falls within the range of a 200 sccm flow controller.

**Important Sizing Notes:**
- When calculating equivalent N₂ flows using gas correction factors, use a flow controller with sufficient flow rate range
- Example: If calculated equivalent N₂ flow is 205 sccm, use a 500 sccm flow controller
- The 500 sccm instrument can then be calibrated such that 205 sccm N₂ = full scale

**WARNING NOTE:** When using a gas with a density higher than nitrogen, be sure that the control valve full scale range can accommodate the desired flow rate. Please call the MKS Applications group if you have any questions.

### Configurations for Low or High Differential Pressure
- Mass flow controllers designed to operate under certain differential pressure conditions
- Nominal values listed in Appendix A, Specifications
- Special configurations available on request for applications with low or high pressure drops
- **Typical low pressure application example:** Vaporizer where liquid material is evaporated at low pressure and reasonable low temperature

---

## CHAPTER 10: MAINTENANCE (Pages 73-75)

### General Maintenance Requirements

**After proper installation and correct setup:**
- Typically only need for occasionally checking zero flow signal
- Readjusting zero flow signal if necessary  
- In general no further maintenance required

**Calibration and Valve Check Frequency depends on:**
- Physical influences (temperature, vibrations, dust etc.)
- Required accuracy
- How the process gas affects the wetted parts inside the unit

**Upon Receipt Failure Check:**
- Check for shipping damage
- Check power/signal cable for correct continuity  
- Any damage should be reported to carrier and MKS Instruments immediately

### Zero Adjustment

**Purpose:** To achieve optimum accuracy and reliability, periodically check zero readout and readjust if necessary

**Reference:** For adjusting zero refer to instructions in Chapter 5; Chapter 6, Operation of Analog/Profibus Units

**Frequency:** How often zero readout should be checked and corrected depends on required accuracy and reproducibility the process demands

### Checks and Recalibration

**Service Options:**
- Checks and recalibrations can be done by any service center of MKS (refer to section Repair)
- **Recommended:** Annual maintenance and recalibration at MKS service center if nothing else specified or shorter intervals necessary

**Equipment Available:**
- MKS offers many standard equipment for checking and calibrating mass flow meters/controllers
- Allows testing and calibration even in situ

**Electronic Testing Recommendations:**
- Do all measurements directly at interface connector of unit
- Eliminates or detects erratic diagnosis typically generated by incorrect grounding
- **MKS Breakout Connectors:** Switched between connector of unit and cable, provide test pin for each wire, allowing direct access for volt meter or oscilloscope etc.

### Profibus Support Kit

**Part Number:** 1179-PB-SUPPORT

**Kit Contents:**
- 1 Disc 3.5"
- 1 RS 232 cable  
- 1 Converter RS232/RS485
- 1 Instruction paper

**Purpose:** Allows convenient setup and diagnosis of Profibus mass flow meter/controller

### Customer Support

**Service Availability:** 
- Standard maintenance and repair services available at all regional MKS Calibration and Service Centers (listed at end of manual)
- MKS accepts instruments of other manufacturers for recalibration using Primary and Transfer Standard calibration equipment located at all regional service centers

**Support Contact:**
- For difficulties with type 1179 instrument
- To obtain information about companion products MKS offers  
- Contact any authorized MKS Calibration and Service Center

**Return Requirements:**
- If necessary to return instrument to MKS, the MKS Calibration and Service Center will inform about formal requirements
- List of MKS Calibration and Service Centers and form for Declaration of Contamination at end of handbook

**⚠️ WARNING:** All returns to MKS Instruments must be free of harmful, corrosive, radioactive, or toxic materials.

---

## TROUBLESHOOTING TABLE (Page 75)

### Complete Troubleshooting Matrix

| **Symptoms** | **Possible Cause** | **Remedy** |
|--------------|-------------------|------------|
| **No output or overrange at zero (after warm-up)** | Improper cable | Check cable for type |
| | Valve override function applied (Mass flow controller) | Disconnect / disable valve override |
| | Electronics malfunctioning | Return for service |
| **Unit indicates a negative flow** | Unit installed in gas stream backwards | Reinstall unit in proper flow direction |
| **Controller does not track set point** | Improper zero adjustment | Zero meter output |
| | Improper grounding(s) | Check all ground connections. Check signals, if possible directly at the unit's connector |
| **Controller does not function** | Electronics malfunctioning | Return for service |
| | Valve sticking, clogged, contaminated, corroded | Check compatibility of the process gas with materials wetted (corrosion is typically also visible inside the process fittings). Readjust the valve (return for service) |
| | Shutoff valve upstream or downstream closed | Open shutoff valve first, then apply again setpoint to the unit |
| | No inlet pressure | Regulate inlet pressure |
| **Oscillation** | Supply pressure unstable, e.g. defective pressure regulator | Check manufacturers' specifications |
| | Supply pressure too high | Reduce upstream pressure |

### Document References
- **Model Numbers:** 1179B/1479B/2179B/179B
- **Page Numbers:** 71, 72, 73, 74, 75
- **Referenced Chapters:** Chapter 5, Chapter 6 (Operation of Analog/Profibus Units)
- **Referenced Appendices:** Appendix A (Specifications), Appendix C (Gas Correction Factors)

### Key Technical Parameters
- **Gas Correction Factor for Nitrogen:** 1.00
- **Gas Correction Factor for Argon:** 1.39
- **Calculation Formula:** (x) = (Gas flow rate) × GCF(N₂) ÷ GCF(Gas)
- **Standard Reference Gas:** Nitrogen (N₂)
- **Flow Rate Units:** sccm (standard cubic centimeters per minute)

**Gas**: SiH4 - **Temperature**: Flat profile - **610°C**: Fine grain formation - **650°C**: Coarse grain formation ### 4.2.2 DESCRIPTION #### 4.2.2.1 Purpose Flat poly-Si process used in situations with strong demand for exactly dimensioned grain structures: - **As diffusion source**: Requires small grains (more grain boundaries to diffuse along) - **As "bulk material" in Thin Film Transistor (TFT) applications**: Grain structure affects mobility of charge carriers (larger grain = less grain boundaries = less barriers = higher mobility) #### 4.2.2.2 Chemicals **SiH4 (Silane)**: - **Classification**: Pyrogenic gas - **Properties**: Burns spontaneously when contacts O2 or air - **Toxicity**: Toxic, but generally burns before becoming toxic #### 4.2.2.3 Process - **Temperature profile**: Flat - **Gas consumption**: SiH4 depletion occurs - **Result**: Thickness decreases along the load **Improvement Methods**: 1. **High flow dilution**: Use high flow of inert gases to dilute SiH4 and reduce consumption - ## SECTION 4.1-9: TROUBLESHOOTING TABLE

### Process Issues and Solutions:

**Issue: The paddle is colder than the wafers and the tube**
- Solution: Insert temperature stabilization step in the recipe after 'evacuate' and/or 'heat up' step

**Issue: Wafers are thick at the edges and thin at the center**
- Causes:
  - Temperature is not homogeneous within wafer
  - Process pressure is too high
- Solutions:
  - Insert temperature stabilization step in the recipe after 'boat in' and/or 'heat up' step
  - Decrease process pressure

**Issue: No consistency in uniformity**
- Causes:
  - Dirty quartz ware or wafers
  - No constant pressure control
  - Draft along the furnace
- Solutions:
  - Clean tube, quartz ware and paddle
  - Use clean wafers
  - Check pressure control
  - Decrease draft by reducing overpressure cleanroom or close possible draft holes

**Issue: Layer is too thin cross load**
- Causes:
  - Deposition time is too short
  - Pressure is too low
- Solutions:
  - Increase deposition time in recipe
  - Increase pressure

**Issue: Layer is too thick cross load**
- Causes:
  - Deposition time is too long
  - Pressure is too high
- Solutions:
  - Decrease deposition time in recipe
  - Decrease pressure

This document describes the various procedures required for a process engineer. The procedures describe all information according to the process engineers.

**5.1 Load or Unload process wafers**
Loading and unloading process wafers on the paddle is required before and after each process run.

**CAUTION**

• Allow the wafers to cool down.

### Section 2 - TECHNICAL DATA

#### 2.1 Operating and storage conditions
- **Ambient temperature range (operation)**: 12 to 40°C
- **Normal surface temperature of the pump-body at ultimate vacuum (operation) ambient temperature of 20°C**: 45 to 65°C
- **Maximum humidity (operation)**: 90%RH
- **Ambient temperature range (storage)**: -30 to 70°C

#### 2.2 Electrical data: single-phase motors
The electrical data for single-phase motors is given in Table 1. The motor start-up current is drawn for less than one second, so you must use slow-blow fuses to prevent unnecessary fuse failure when the pump starts. If you use the pump at temperatures lower than 12°C, the start-up current will be drawn for longer; this may cause the motor thermal overload device to open.

#### 2.3 Electrical data: three-phase motors
The electrical data for three-phase motors is given in Table 2. The motor start-up current is drawn for less than one second, so you must use slow-blow fuses to prevent unnecessary fuse failure when the pump starts.

#### 2.4 Lubrication data
**Note**: An Edwards Health and Safety Data Sheet for the following oil is available on request.

- **Recommended oil***: Ultragrade 19
- **Maximum oil capacity**:
  - 0.6 litre (E1M5 or 8)
  - 0.55 litre (E2M2, 5 or 8)
  - 0.8 litre (E2M12)

*To operate the pump when the ambient temperature is outside the range specified in Section 2.1, or to optimise the pump performance when you process condensable vapours, you may need a different oil.

## 3.9 Outlet connection

### WARNING BOX:
**WARNING**
Connect the pump outlet to a suitable treatment plant to prevent the discharge of dangerous gases and vapours to the surrounding atmosphere. Use a catchpot to prevent the drainage of contaminated condensate back into the pump.

### Connection Methods:
To connect the pump to your outlet accessories or to your exhaust treatment plant, you can:

• Connect 10 mm internal diameter vacuum hose or 12 mm internal diameter plastic hose to the outlet nozzle (Figure 1, item 1).

• Remove the outlet nozzle and connect to the 3/4 inch BSP threaded hole.

• Remove the outlet nozzle and replace it with an NW25 flange adaptor (available as an optional accessory, see Section 7) and then connect to the NW25 flange.

### Technical Requirements and Specifications:

Take note of the following information before you connect to the pump outlet:

• The exhaust system must be configured so that the maximum pressure at the pump outlet does not exceed **0.5 bar gauge (1.5 bar absolute, 1.5 x 10⁵ Pa)** at full pump throughput.

### Oil Mist Filter Recommendations:
• In the following circumstances, we recommend that you fit an oil mist filter to the pump outlet:
  • if you use the pump with the gas-ballast control open
  • if you operate the pump with an inlet pressure greater than **10 mbar** for extended periods or
  • if you frequently pump down from atmospheric pressure.

The mist filter will trap the oil exhausted from the pump: you can re-use the oil if it is not contaminated.

### Additional Requirements (from top of page):
• Use a suitable catchpot if you pump condensable vapours or if you use the pump for very dusty applications.
• Ensure that sealing surfaces are clean and scratch-free.

### Model Information:
**E2M2, E1/E2M5 and 8, E2M12 Rotary Vacuum Pumps**

**4.5 To pump condensable vapours**
- Use gas-ballast (open the gas-ballast control) when there is a high proportion of condensable vapours in the process gases
- Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Close the vacuum system isolation-valve
  2. Turn the gas-ballast control anti-clockwise to fully open and operate the pump for 30 minutes to warm the oil; this will help to prevent vapour condensation in the pump
  3. Open the vacuum system isolation-valve and continue to operate the pump with the gas-ballast control open
- Note: After pumping condensable vapours, you can (if necessary) decontaminate the oil using procedure in Section 4.6

**4.6 To decontaminate the oil**
- The oil in the pump should be clear; if the oil is cloudy or discoloured, it is contaminated with process vapours
- Procedure:
  1. Look at the condition of the oil in the oil sight-glass (Figure 1, item 12). If the oil is cloudy or discoloured, continue with Step 2
  2. Close the vacuum system isolation-valve
  3. Turn the gas-ballast control fully anti-clockwise
  4. Operate the pump until the oil is clear

**4.7 Unattended operation**
- The pump is designed for unattended operation under normal operating conditions specified in Section 2
- Recommended check interval: not more than 14 days
- Check more frequently if:
  - Pumping high volumes of gas
  - Operating the pump with the gas-ballast control open

### ALARM SIGNAL TYPES

**Two Different Audible Alarm Signals:**

1. **Operator Alert Signal:**
   - Sound pattern: Intermittent "beep-beep-beep"
   - Purpose: Alert operator for action (example: press 'Start')

2. **Alarm Situation Signal:**
   - Sound pattern: Continuous "beeeeeeeeeeeeep"
   - Purpose: Alert for alarm situation

**Operator Alert Signals (beep-beep-beep):**
- Requirement: Operator must press 'Start' to have the furnace proceed to the next step in the process

## SECTION 5.6 - PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE PROCESSES

### DAILY MAINTENANCE:
**Checklist Items:**
- Check system for any alarms during process and/or standby
- Check system and/or logging for base pressure value. (This will indicate that with any major rise the vacuum pump and/or line can have problems)
- Check the vacuum atmosphere switch, adjust to atmospheric pressure. LED should light up
- [Two blank checklist items with dashed lines]

### WEEKLY MAINTENANCE:
**Checklist Items:**
- Check the pump status
- If applicable, check TEOS system bubbler temperature
- Check the tightness of the vacuumline connections:
  • Front flange screws
  • Ball joint clamp
- Check the waterflow on the flanges and the heatexchanger
- [Two blank checklist items with dashed lines]

### MONTHLY MAINTENANCE:
**Checklist Items:**
- Replace the O-ring on the Loaddoor
- [Two blank checklist items with dashed lines]

### YEARLY MAINTENANCE:
**Checklist Items:**
- Clean complete vacuum lines of the system
- Full check on the vacuum pumps
- [Two blank checklist items with dashed lines]

## SECTION 6 - TROUBLE SHOOTING

### TROUBLESHOOTING TABLE:

**PROBLEM 1: Pressure is low after the gassystem**
- **POSSIBLE CAUSES:**
  • Check regulator setting; check if the gas supply valves are open
  • Filters are clogged
  • The gas cylinder is empty
  • MFC is out of order
- **SOLUTIONS:**
  • Replace filter
  • Replace gas cylinder
  • Replace MFC

**PROBLEM 2: No flow**
- **POSSIBLE CAUSES:**
  • Valve is closed
  • Regulator is out of order
  • MFC is out of order
- **SOLUTIONS:**
  • Replace valve
  • Check safety condition and replace regulator
  • Replace MFC

### ADDITIONAL TROUBLESHOOTING NOTES:
"In most cases (especially at N2 and Airflow) the pressure will be checked by the pressureswitches. If a failure appears, the DPC will give an alarm. For more information, look at the manual of the DPC.

If a gasflow reduces and a high-low limit is set, an alarm is activated by the DPC."
